content:
The Section 301 review is done and most of the List 3 rates are staying put. If you have been waiting for relief on the 25 percent line items, stop waiting and plan around them.

Two things worth your time. First, check whether any of your HTS numbers landed on the new exclusion list. A handful of machinery and parts codes did. Second, if you are paying 301 duties on goods you could source from Mexico or a third country, run the math now, not at renewal.

Nobody is getting a refund here. The work is forward looking. Pull your last twelve months of entries, sort by 301 duty paid, and start at the top of that list.
excerpt: USTR finished its four-year review of the Section 301 China tariffs. Here is what actually changed and what to do about it.
title: Section 301 List 3 review: what the new tranche means for importers

content:
Most people sourcing automotive parts know the regional value content thresholds. Fewer track the labor value content requirement, and that is the one that catches them.

If you certify origin without documentation behind the labor value content, you are signing something you cannot back up. When CBP asks, and on autos they do ask, a clean RVC calculation will not save you if the LVC paperwork is missing.

Build the file before you certify. Supplier affidavits, the cost breakdown, and the wage data. If you cannot produce it on request, do not certify.
excerpt: Regional value content is not the only test. The labor value content requirement trips up more importers than the RVC math does.
title: USMCA auto rules of origin: the part people keep missing

content:
The ten plus two filing is not new, but the tolerance for late and sloppy filings has tightened. We are seeing more liquidated damages claims for the same mistakes we have always warned about.

File before the goods load, not after. Get all ten data elements right the first time. If you are missing a container number, hold and call the carrier. Do not file incomplete and amend, because that is exactly how you draw a penalty.

An ISF bond claim runs five thousand dollars per filing. That is real money for a mistake that takes ten minutes to avoid.
excerpt: CBP has been less forgiving on late and inaccurate ISF filings this year. A quick reminder on the basics that keep you out of trouble.
title: ISF penalties are climbing. File on time and file complete.

content:
The de minimis exemption lets shipments under 800 dollars enter duty free with minimal paperwork. There is real momentum in Washington to narrow it, especially for goods from China.

If your supply chain runs on small parcel imports under the threshold, do not assume it stays. Model what your landed cost looks like if those shipments suddenly owe duty and need a formal entry.

I am not predicting the date. I am saying have the answer ready before someone asks you for it in a hurry.
excerpt: The 800 dollar de minimis threshold is getting a hard look in Washington. If your model depends on it, start planning now.
title: De minimis is under pressure. Watch this one closely.

content:
The Section 232 duties on steel and aluminum have been part of the landscape since 2018. What changes is the derivatives list and which countries are in or out.

If you import anything with steel or aluminum content, even as a component, check whether the derivative product rules pull you in. People assume 232 is only raw metal. It is not.

Country exclusions and quotas shift. Verify the current status for your origin before you assume the rate. Last year's answer is not this year's answer.
excerpt: The 232 tariffs on steel and aluminum are not going anywhere. A short note on derivatives and the country exclusions that actually apply.
title: Section 232 steel and aluminum duties: still here, still matter

content:
The Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act puts a rebuttable presumption on goods tied to the Xinjiang region. If CBP detains your shipment, the burden is on you to prove the supply chain is clean.

That means a full supply chain map, not a one line supplier statement. Where the cotton was grown. Where the polysilicon was refined. Documentation back to the raw material, with dates and locations.

If you import textiles, solar, or electronics and you do not have this file ready, you are one detention away from a stuck container and a buyer who is done waiting. Build the map before you need it.
excerpt: Forced labor enforcement under UFLPA is not slowing down. Here is the documentation that gets your shipment released.
title: UFLPA detentions: what you need on file before the goods ship

content:
The updated HTS took effect this year with the usual mix of new subheadings, deleted codes, and rate changes. If you are still entering goods under last year's number, some of you are now wrong.

Pull your active classifications and check each one against the current schedule. A deleted or changed subheading does not get fixed by ignoring it. It gets fixed when CBP catches it, and that is the expensive version.

This is an afternoon of work that saves you a penalty case. Do it once a year, every year, right after the revisions publish.
excerpt: The Harmonized Tariff Schedule gets revised every year. A reminder to verify your classifications instead of rolling them forward on autopilot.
title: The annual HTS revisions are out. Recheck your codes.

content:
Antidumping and countervailing deposit rates are not fixed. Commerce reviews them, and when a new rate publishes, your cash deposit obligation changes from that date forward.

If you import goods under an active AD/CVD order, you need to track the case. A rate jump from single digits to triple digits is not rare in these reviews, and it applies to entries after the effective date whether you saw it coming or not.

Know your case number. Know your producer's specific rate, not just the all others rate. Those are usually very different numbers.
excerpt: Commerce issued new deposit rates in several active AD/CVD cases. If you import covered goods, your cash deposit may have changed overnight.
title: Antidumping deposit rates moved. Check whether yours did.

content:
Drawback lets you recover up to 99 percent of the duties you paid on imported goods that you later export or destroy. It is one of the few places in customs law where the money flows back to you.

The reason most companies do not claim it is that the recordkeeping looks intimidating. It is not trivial, but if you are moving real volume the refund is worth the file. We have seen clients recover five and six figures a year.

You can claim back five years. If you have been exporting product you paid duty to import and never filed, you are leaving cash behind. Run the numbers.
excerpt: If you import and then export, or destroy, you may be owed a refund of duties paid. Most eligible companies never file.
title: Duty drawback: money a lot of importers leave on the table

content:
Every winter the St. Lawrence Seaway and the Soo Locks close, and Great Lakes vessel traffic stops until spring. The exact dates shift a little each year, but the close is coming.

If you have cargo that needs to move by water through the lakes, the deadline is the lock closure, not your delivery date. Miss it and your options are an expensive reroute or a long wait.

Living in Ohio, I have watched importers get surprised by this every December. Do not be one of them. Check the closure schedule and book accordingly.
excerpt: The Soo Locks and the Seaway close for winter. If you move cargo through the Great Lakes, the calendar matters more than the rate this time of year.
title: Great Lakes shipping season is winding down. Plan around the close.

content:
Start of the year, so here is what is actually on my radar. Not predictions, just the things most likely to cost you money if you are not paying attention.

One, de minimis. The pressure to narrow the 800 dollar threshold has not gone away. Two, Section 301. Expect more exclusion churn and possibly new actions. Three, UFLPA enforcement keeps widening to new commodities.

If you import, pick the one of these that touches your goods and get ahead of it this quarter. You cannot watch all three. Watch yours.
excerpt: A short, honest read on the tariff and trade issues most likely to hit importers this year. No predictions, just the live wires.
title: New year tariff outlook: what I am watching in 2026

content:
A USMCA claim is a statement that you can document the good qualifies. CBP has been testing that more often, and a verification letter is not a suggestion. It is a deadline.

When the letter arrives you have a fixed window to produce the origin records. If your supplier affidavits are stale or your rule of origin analysis lives in someone's head, that window is going to feel very short.

Pull your USMCA claims now and confirm each one has a current file behind it. Do it before the letter, not after.
excerpt: CBP has been sending more origin verification requests on USMCA claims. If you certified, you need to be able to prove it.
title: USMCA verification letters are going out. Be ready to answer.

content:
Looking at the penalty cases crossing my desk, the pattern is consistent. Most are not exotic. They are classification errors and valuation problems that a basic review would have caught.

Misclassified goods that underpaid duty. Assists and royalties left out of declared value. First sale claims with no documentation. None of this is new law. It is people not doing the homework.

The fix is boring and it works. Review your top classifications, confirm your declared value includes everything it should, and keep the file. A prior disclosure on your own terms beats a penalty notice on theirs.
excerpt: A look at where the penalty cases are concentrating this year. Classification and valuation, mostly. The avoidable stuff.
title: Customs penalty trends: what CBP is actually targeting

content:
Short one this time. The Columbus trade mentorship program that Ryan Montoya and I run has a new cohort starting this spring. Watching newer brokers work through their first real classification problems is the most fun I have in this job.

This blog has also been going for a couple of years now, two posts a quarter, all of it on whatever tariff or compliance issue is keeping importers up at night. Thanks for reading it and for the questions in the comments. Keep them coming.

Back to regular tariff coverage next time.
excerpt: A quick firm note. The Columbus trade mentorship program has a new cohort, and the blog hits a small milestone.
title: KTS mentorship program: spring cohort and a thank you

content:
This is a working draft I want to get right before it goes live. The question I get most often is whether tariff programs stack, and the short answer is yes, they often do.

The same imported good can owe a Section 301 duty, a Section 232 duty, and an antidumping or countervailing duty all at once, on top of the regular HTS rate. People see one rate, plan around it, and get blindsided by the total.

I want to add a clean worked walkthrough with real numbers before publishing so the math is obvious. Holding this until that is solid.
excerpt: Working draft. A walkthrough of what happens when Section 301, Section 232, and antidumping duties all land on the same entry.
title: Tariff stacking: when more than one duty hits the same import

content:
Draft in progress. A continuous customs bond is sized to your duty volume, and when that volume jumps, your bond can go insufficient without you doing anything wrong.

This is hitting importers right now because tariff increases raise the duties you owe, which raises the bond CBP requires. The first sign is often an insufficiency notice that holds your entries until you post more.

I want to add the actual sufficiency formula and a couple of real thresholds before this publishes. Not done yet.
excerpt: Working draft. Notes on continuous bond sufficiency and why a tariff increase can force an insufficiency notice you did not expect.
title: When to upsize your customs bond before CBP makes you

content:
Every year I post the same checklist because every year it saves somebody a January headache. This is the 2026 edition, scheduled to go up before the holiday slowdown.

Reconcile your entries against your duty payments. File any drawback claims before they age out. Confirm your continuous bond is still sufficient for next year's projected volume. Recheck your HTS codes against the revisions that take effect January 1.

None of this is hard. All of it is easier in October than in a December panic. Do the boring work now and start the year clean.
excerpt: Scheduled for fall. The annual run-through of what to close out before December 31 so January does not start with a problem.
title: Year-end compliance checklist for importers, 2026 edition

content:
A foreign-trade zone lets you bring goods into the country without paying duty until they leave the zone for the U.S. market. If you re-export, you may never pay it at all. For the right importer that is a real cash-flow swing.

The catch is the activation and recordkeeping. An FTZ is not a warehouse you rent and forget. You run an inventory system CBP can audit, and you file an annual reconciliation. Done sloppily it costs you more in compliance than it saves in duty.

If you hold inventory, re-export a meaningful share, or pay duty on goods that sit before they sell, run the numbers on a zone. If you turn product fast and sell it all domestically, it is probably not worth the overhead.
excerpt: Foreign-trade zones defer or eliminate duty for some importers. They also add real recordkeeping. Here is how to tell if a zone is worth it for you.
title: Foreign-trade zones: a duty break, or just more paperwork?

content:
First sale valuation lets you declare customs value on the price of the first sale in a multi-tier transaction, not the last one you paid. When there is a middleman markup between the factory and you, that difference is dutiable value you may not owe.

It is legitimate and long-settled, but it is not automatic. You need the factory invoice, proof the goods were destined for export to the U.S. at the first sale, and an arm's-length price. Miss any leg of that and the claim does not hold.

If you buy through a trading company or a related party and your duty bill is large, this is worth a serious look. The savings scale with your volume and your duty rate, and right now both are up.
excerpt: First sale valuation can cut the dutiable value on goods you buy through a middleman. It is legal and underused, but the documentation has to be airtight.
title: First sale valuation: paying duty on the right number

content:
A binding ruling from CBP tells you, in writing, how a specific good will be classified or valued before you import it. Once you have it, CBP is bound by it. That is the whole point: certainty instead of a guess that gets second-guessed at liquidation.

People skip this because it feels slow. It does take a few weeks. But if you are about to import a new product line at volume and the classification is genuinely arguable, a ruling is cheap insurance against a reclassification that hits every entry going back.

Write the request carefully. A vague description gets you a vague ruling you cannot rely on. Describe the good, propose your classification, and explain your reasoning. We draft these for clients regularly and the quality of the request drives the quality of the answer.
excerpt: When a classification is genuinely arguable, a binding ruling from CBP gives you certainty before the goods ever ship. Here is when it is worth requesting.
title: Binding rulings: get the answer in writing before you import

content:
Scheduled for the fall. The Great Lakes navigation season opens again in spring, and every year importers who move bulk cargo by water plan around the lock and Seaway dates without thinking through the duty side.

The point I want to make: the timing of when your goods arrive and clear sets when the duty is owed, and a tariff change between order and arrival can move that number. If you booked a lakes vessel months out, the rate you modeled may not be the rate you pay.

I will post the season dates and a short checklist for tying your entry plan to the shipping calendar once the schedule firms up. Living in Ohio, I watch this every year, and the cargo that gets stranded is always the cargo nobody planned around.
excerpt: Scheduled for the fall. Tying your customs entry plan to the Great Lakes shipping calendar, and why a tariff change mid-voyage can change your duty bill.
title: Great Lakes shipping season and the duty timing nobody plans for
